# Search And Retrieval

## Summary

The first retrieval layer is not vector search. It is good page structure, disciplined source notes, and a clear default start path.

Use tiered reading so agents open the smallest useful set of files instead of turning the wiki into a long context dump.

## Default Stack

- Obsidian internal links
- backlinks
- graph view
- properties
- local markdown files

## Tiered Read Depth Model

Use this model for normal wiki lookup:

- Tier 0: orientation files only: nearest `AGENTS.md`, vault `AGENTS.md`, [[Task Routing]], and repo-local docs named by the task.
- Tier 1: one lead wiki page from the matching route card.
- Tier 2: one or two support pages only when the lead page or route card names a specific trigger.
- Tier 3: `raw/` source notes, official docs, screenshots, broad local search, or web research only when the task needs citations, current facts, source-quality review, or high-stakes accuracy.

Tier 3 is evidence mode. Do not use it for ordinary routing when the maintained wiki page already gives enough operational guidance.

## Retrieval Order

When looking for an answer in this vault:

1. start with [[Task Routing]] for task-led lookup
2. open the Tier 1 lead page from the matching route card
3. use [[Index]] only when the topic family is known but the route card is not enough
4. follow the smallest useful set of linked Tier 2 support pages
5. prefer source notes in `raw/` when the claim needs evidence
6. use local text search only after the page structure and links stop being enough

The goal is to retrieve the right page, not to search the whole vault every time.

Default retrieval scope is `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, `wiki/`, and `raw/` source notes. Treat `tmp/` as scratch/reference material and exclude it from normal search unless a maintained page explicitly points there.

When a run needs usage evidence, report the pages used, retrieval tier, estimated wiki tokens, route hit, and context economy through [[Wiki Context Reporting]].

## Query Discipline

- search for task language, workflow names, source-note titles, and specific standards first
- prefer exact page names or strong noun phrases over vague keywords
- if the task is routed already, open the routed pages before freeform searching
- if the answer depends on evidence, read the source note instead of trusting a summary alone
- avoid broad recursive search through `tmp/`; promote durable findings into `raw/` or `wiki/` before relying on them
- if the page structure is weak, fix the headings and summary before reaching for heavier retrieval machinery
- if a Tier 1 page answers the task, stop reading and proceed to execution or verification

## Stop Conditions

Stop expanding context when all of these are true:

- the source hierarchy is clear
- the task family is classified
- one lead workflow or standard owns the work
- the capability choice is clear enough to use or skip a skill, plugin, app, or automation
- the likely files or systems are identified
- the verification path is known

Do not keep reading because another related page exists. Keep deeper pages available as support, not default context.

## Structural Retrieval Rules

- use exact page names and stable section headings
- keep page summaries short and high-signal
- keep the strongest retrieval cues near the top of the page
- do not bury critical guidance in the middle of long undifferentiated pages
- prefer topic hubs and comparison pages for cross-page questions
- use route-card language on task maps so agents can distinguish lead pages from optional support pages

## Signs The Current Layer Is Still Enough

- the relevant page can usually be found from [[Index]] or a small number of links
- source notes are easy to trace from the page that uses them
- agents are not repeatedly searching the whole vault for the same topics
- page names map cleanly to recurring task types
- route cards usually identify one lead page without needing broad search

## Signs Retrieval Is Becoming A Bottleneck

- agents repeatedly miss existing pages
- too many sibling pages overlap on the same concept
- finding the right source note takes broad keyword search instead of clear navigation
- the answer depends on scanning too many long pages rather than following a narrow path
- scratch or vendored folders keep appearing in search results for normal wiki questions
- route cards list too many pages without lead and trigger labels

## When To Add More

If the vault grows enough that plain browse and search stop being reliable, add a stronger retrieval layer such as a markdown-aware hybrid search system.

Before adding more:

- improve headings, aliases, and summaries
- convert broad page lists into route cards
- improve source-note provenance
- add topic hubs for repeated global questions
- check whether the real failure mode is cross-page synthesis rather than missing search

## Candidate

`qmd` is relevant because it is designed for markdown collections and supports hybrid retrieval rather than plain filename search alone.

If the corpus grows further, hierarchical summary pages are a better next step than graph extraction by default.

## What Not To Do

- do not add vector search just because it sounds advanced
- do not treat graph view as a substitute for clear page naming and linking
- do not keep adding overlapping pages instead of improving navigation
- do not use retrieval to compensate for weak source discipline
- do not treat long context dumps as a substitute for ranked evidence
- do not add graph or vector layers before naming, linking, and provenance are doing their job
- do not pre-load every page that might be relevant when a route card already names a lead page

## Rule

Do not add a heavier retrieval layer until the simpler linked-markdown workflow is actually becoming a bottleneck.
